full_response:
First recall the relation between the Kruskal–Szekeres coordinates (U , V) and the Schwarzschild coordinates (t , r) (we set G=c=1 and write M instead of GM):

1.  Tortoise coordinate
     r_* = r + 2M ln| r/2M – 1 | .

2.  Eddington–Finkelstein null coordinates  
     u = t – r_*,     v = t + r_* .

3.  Kruskal coordinates  
     U = ϵ_U e^{-u/4M},     V = ϵ_V e^{v/4M},     ϵ_U , ϵ_V = ±1 ,

   chosen so that the product  

     UV = –( r/2M – 1 ) e^{r/2M}                (★)

   is valid in every region.

Exterior points have r > 2M, so r/2M – 1 > 0 and therefore (★) gives

                               UV < 0 .                                        (1)

Moreover, as r → ∞ one finds

                    UV ≃ –(r/2M) e^{r/2M}  →  –∞ ;                             (2)

so spatial infinity corresponds to the part of the (U , V)-plane where the product UV is large and negative.

Because UV<0, the signs of U and V must be opposite.  There are therefore two and only two ways of satisfying (1) and (2):

•  U < 0 , V > 0 : “right-hand” exterior (called Region I).  
   Taking r → ∞ at fixed, finite t gives  
   U → –∞ , V → +∞ with UV → –∞ .

•  U > 0 , V < 0 : “left-hand” exterior (called Region III).  
   Again r → ∞ gives  
   U → +∞ , V → –∞ with UV → –∞ .

These two oppositely signed quadrants are the two asymptotically flat ends of the maximally extended Schwarzschild geometry—the two mouths of the Einstein–Rosen bridge (wormhole).

\boxed{\text{Asymptotic region I: } U<0,\; V>0 \quad(\;r\to\infty,\;UV\to-\infty\;)}
\boxed{\text{Asymptotic region III: } U>0,\; V<0 \quad(\;r\to\infty,\;UV\to-\infty\;)}
